region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent; commonly served in Singapore Indian cuisine

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a chapati entry but does not specifically represent chapati served with gravy as a combined dish; closest_parent used for the flatbread component.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP represents chapati (flatbread) without the gravy accompaniment; used as closest parent for the combined dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for chapati with gravy as a combined dish.

preparation_method: served with cooked gravy or curry

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of two chapatis (approximately 60–70 g each) accompanied by approximately 70–80 g of gravy or curry, totalling around 200 g.

nutrient_source.title: Chapati (Phulka) and Dal / Vegetable Curry — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Bread, chapati or roti, whole wheat (FDC ID 175040) adapted with gravy component
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Per-100g values represent a weighted composite of chapati (whole wheat, dry-roasted) and a typical vegetable/lentil-based gravy in approximately 65:35 ratio by weight, consistent with a standard Indian restaurant serving. Chapati macros sourced from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (chapati, phulka); gravy component estimated from ICMR-NIN dal/sabzi entries. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. Sodium elevated to reflect restaurant-style gravy seasoning.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chapati (whole wheat, Indian flatbread) — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for whole wheat chapati (phulka). The combined dish (chapati with gravy) is expected to have a similar or slightly lower GI due to the protein and fat content of the gravy component, which slows gastric emptying. GI of 52 is used as a conservative estimate for the combined dish. GL per serving calculated as: (52 × 32.5 × 200/100) / 100 = 33.8 g.

hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving provides approximately 620 mg sodium (about 27% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt gravy and limit portion size to one chapati with a smaller gravy serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from whole wheat carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Whole wheat chapati has a low GI of approximately 52, but the glycemic load per standard serving (~33.8 g) is significant; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one chapati, pair with a protein-rich or vegetable-based low-GI gravy, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Whole grain fibre and low saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Whole wheat chapati provides approximately 3.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g and relatively low saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g), making it a heart-friendly staple when served with a vegetable-based gravy rather than a cream- or ghee-heavy curry.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chapati with Gravy is a popular Indian meal consisting of chapati — a thin, unleavened whole wheat flatbread (also called phulka or roti) — served alongside a cooked gravy or curry. The chapati is made from whole wheat flour (atta), water, and a small amount of oil or ghee, then dry-roasted on a flat iron griddle (tawa) until lightly charred spots appear. The gravy component varies by restaurant and may be a lentil-based dal, a vegetable curry (sabzi), or a spiced tomato-onion sauce. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at Indian Muslim and South Indian eateries and hawker stalls, often as a breakfast or lunch item. The texture of chapati is soft and slightly chewy with a mild, nutty whole wheat flavour; the gravy adds moisture, spice, and umami depth. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~195 kcal/100 g), meaningful dietary fibre from whole wheat (~3.2 g/100 g), and a low-to-moderate glycemic index (~52 for the chapati component), making it a more nutritionally balanced choice compared to refined-flour breads. Sodium content is elevated in restaurant preparations due to seasoned gravy. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and gravy composition, while those with hypertension should be mindful of sodium from the gravy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chapati with gravy?
What is the GI of chapati?
Is chapati with curry high in sodium?
Is chapati with gravy suitable for weight loss?
Chapati vs roti — what is the difference?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (chapati/phulka) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, with the gravy component estimated from ICMR-NIN dal/sabzi entries in a 65:35 ratio. No exact combined-dish entry for 'chapati with gravy' was found in a single approved primary source. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for whole wheat chapati and applied as an analogous estimate for the combined dish. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a weighted composite of chapati (whole wheat, dry-roasted, ~65% by weight) and a generic vegetable/lentil gravy (~35% by weight), consistent with a standard restaurant serving. Exact gravy composition varies by restaurant. 2. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 3. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in the standard vegetarian preparation; cholesterol is 0.0 (confirmed zero) for the same reason. Vitamin D is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as neither whole wheat chapati nor vegetable gravy contains meaningful vitamin D per ICMR-NIN and USDA sources. 4. Trans fat is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional chapati preparation. 5. GI of 52 is from the University of Sydney GI Database for whole wheat chapati; the combined dish GI may be slightly lower due to the protein and fat in the gravy. 6. Sodium of 310 mg/100 g reflects restaurant-style seasoned gravy; home-prepared versions would be lower. 7. is_vegan is false because some preparations use ghee (clarified butter); is_vegetarian is true for the standard vegetable/lentil gravy version. 8. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302050 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP represent chapati (flatbread) without the gravy component; both used as closest_parent for the combined dish.
